one_line_takeaway: Distributing a single-turn harmful task across multiple, individually-benign-looking turns reliably raises attack success rates on tool-using LLM agents (+16% ASR on average across six frontier/open models), and a training-free self-exploration defense (ToolShield) that has the agent simulate tool misuse before deployment and inject distilled 'safety experiences' cuts that ASR by 30% on average, substantially outperforming a guardrail baseline (LlamaFirewall) and a documentation-only ablation.
key_finding: Multi-turn distribution reliably degrades agent safety: average ASR increases by 16% across models (paper's headline number), with per-model increases of +27.1% (Claude-4.5-Sonnet), +23.0% (Qwen3-Coder), +14.2% (GPT-5.2), +13.4% (Gemini-3-Flash), +9.9% (Seed-1.6), +8.8% (Deepseek-v3.2) (Figure 6/Section 4). Capability does not track with safety: Gemini-3-Flash and GPT-5.2 sit close on a capability index but show a 79% vs 51% multi-turn ASR gap, and Deepseek-v3.2 -- state-of-the-art among open models on capability benchmarks -- has the worst safety (88.57% ASR). Environment-state-targeting attacks (Decomposition x Env) are most effective (73.7% ASR), followed by Addition x Data (72.2%) and Addition x Env (69.9%), with Decomposition x Data lowest (67.9%) (Figure 8). ToolShield reduces multi-turn ASR by 30% on average, with per-model reductions of -49.9pp (Claude-4.5-Sonnet, 72%->22%), -37.5pp (Seed-1.6), -29.9pp (Deepseek-v3.2), -23.8pp (Qwen3-Coder), -19.2pp (Gemini-3-Flash), -18.6pp (GPT-5.2) (Figure 10). Against baselines on 100 sampled tasks, LlamaFirewall only cuts ASR by ~3%, 'ToolShield w/o simulation' is intermediate, and full ToolShield achieves the lowest ASR (88%->15% for Claude-4.5-Sonnet, 81%->57% for Gemini-3-Flash) (Figure 12) -- showing simulated execution traces, not just documentation reading, are what drive effectiveness. Safety experiences transfer across models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Sonnet-generated experiences give the highest average ASR reduction across executors: 50% single-turn, 41% multi-turn) and defense effectiveness scales with generation budget (Claude-4.5-Sonnet: 63%->73% multi-turn ASR reduction as cost rises $0.16->$1.06; Seed-1.6 reaches 38% reduction at $0.19/experience). On 170 benign tasks, ToolShield introduces no rejections (RR stays 0%), indicating no measured over-refusal false-positive cost. Repeated-run analysis (n=3, temp 0.7, 100-task subset, Claude-4.5-Sonnet) shows single-turn ASR 43.30%+/-1.25, multi-turn 88.00%+/-2.16, multi-turn+ToolShield 16.67%+/-2.62, i.e., the reported degradation and defense effects are well outside measured run-to-run variance.
core_contribution: The paper makes three own contributions, clearly separated from cited prior work in its Related Work / Table 1 comparison: (1) a principled Multi-Turn Attack Taxonomy (MAT) with two transformation Formats (Addition, Decomposition), each with two Methods (Mapping/Wrapping for Addition; Composition/Identity for Decomposition), crossed with two Targets (Data Files vs. Environment States), yielding 8 subcategories that convert a single-turn harmful task T into a benign-looking multi-turn attack sequence while preserving the original harmful objective; (2) MT-AgentRisk, built by applying this taxonomy (via an LLM transformer, Claude-4.5-Sonnet at temperature 0) to 365 single-turn harmful tasks curated from four external benchmarks/tasks sources (OpenAgentSafety, SafeArena, P2SQL, MCPMark) across five tools (Playwright-MCP, Terminal, Filesystem-MCP, PostgreSQL-MCP, Notion-MCP), which the paper states is the first benchmark to jointly evaluate multi-turn harm distribution AND tool-usage (per their own Table 1 comparison against MHJ, Haicosystem, SafeDialBench, RedTeamCUA, SafeArena, OpenAgentSafety, MCP-Safety); and (3) ToolShield, a training-free, tool-agnostic self-exploration defense with four stages (test-case synthesis via risk reasoning over tool docs, simulated sandbox execution, experience generation from trajectories, and incremental experience-list update) whose distilled 'safety experiences' are injected into the agent's context at deployment time.
threat_model: Adversary crafts multi-turn instruction sequences to a tool-using LLM agent (with access to real tools such as filesystem, browser/Playwright, terminal, SQL database, or Notion via MCP) such that each individual turn appears benign and would not trigger refusal, but the harmful objective is either (a) reconstructed via an added indirection layer (aliasing/symbolic mapping, or wrapping the payload in a container/conditional structure triggered later) or (b) assembled from decomposed subtasks executed sequentially (explicit composition/merging, or implicit accumulation via environment-state mutation). The manipulated surface is either data files (explicit payloads) or environment states (shell history, variables, runtime state). The paper does not consider adversarial prompt injection from external/untrusted content as the primary axis studied in the main benchmark (some appendix material touches injection scaling as a turn-count-increase mechanism, not as an attacker-controlled external channel) -- the primary threat model is a user/attacker directly issuing the distributed instruction sequence to the agent.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include a dedicated 'Limitations' section in the portion reviewed; the following are gaps evident from the methodology as described. (1) The attack-sequence generation itself is LLM-authored (Claude-4.5-Sonnet as the primary decomposer), so benchmark coverage is bounded by that model's creativity/biases, though the authors do cross-check with Qwen3-Coder as an alternate decomposer per Appendix D.3 (not verified in this text). (2) The LLM-as-Judge (GPT-4.1) grounds success/failure classification; agreement rates (95.15% rule-based, 93.53% human) leave a nontrivial disagreement tail unquantified in the main text. (3) 74% of tasks that were neither rejected nor completed are attributed to 'technical errors during execution rather than safety behaviors' per the authors' own analysis, meaning a sizeable fraction of non-success is not cleanly attributable to safety mechanisms. (4) ToolShield's safety experiences are described as 'static once generated' and injected at deployment -- the defense's robustness to attack sequences specifically crafted knowing the defense pipeline (adaptive/white-box adversaries) is not addressed in the sections reviewed. (5) Benchmark tools are limited to five specific tool types/MCP servers; generalization to arbitrary or novel tool ecosystems beyond these is asserted ('tool-agnostic') but tested only within this fixed tool set.

datasets_used: MT-AgentRisk (this paper's own benchmark): 365 multi-turn attack tasks built from 365 single-turn harmful tasks across 5 tools
OpenAgentSafety (source of Terminal, Filesystem-MCP tasks and some Playwright/web tasks)
SafeArena (source of Playwright/web tasks: GitLab, OwnCloud, Reddit, Shopping, Shopping Admin)
MCPMark (source of Notion-MCP tasks, originally benign, manually converted to harmful by the authors)
quantitative_results: Average multi-turn ASR increase across models: +16%
Per-model ASR increase single->multi-turn: Claude-4.5-Sonnet +27.1%, Qwen3-Coder +23.0%, GPT-5.2 +14.2%, Gemini-3-Flash +13.4%, Seed-1.6 +9.9%, Deepseek-v3.2 +8.8%
Deepseek-v3.2 absolute multi-turn ASR: 88.57% (worst safety despite SOTA open-model capability)
Gemini-3-Flash vs GPT-5.2 multi-turn ASR: 79% vs 51%
ASR by taxonomy category (avg across models): Decomposition x Env 73.7%, Addition x Data 72.2%, Addition x Env 69.9%, Decomposition x Data 67.9%
Per-model ToolShield ASR reduction (percentage points): Claude-4.5-Sonnet -49.9 (72%->22%), Seed-1.6 -37.5, Deepseek-v3.2 -29.9, Qwen3-Coder -23.8, Gemini-3-Flash -19.2, GPT-5.2 -18.6
ToolShield vs baselines on 100 sampled tasks: LlamaFirewall reduces ASR by only ~3%; ToolShield reduces Claude-4.5-Sonnet ASR 88%->15% and Gemini-3-Flash ASR 81%->57%
Single-turn ASR reduction with ToolShield: Claude-4.5-Sonnet -35% (conclusion states -50% multi-turn / -35% single-turn); Qwen3-Coder -24%; Seed-1.6 -38%
Natural Scaling (10-turn attacks): ASR drops from 58.6% to 11.5% with ToolShield
Expert-decomposed attacks: ToolShield reduces ASR from 93.0% to 18.0%
Benign task performance (Claude-4.5-Sonnet, Table 3): w/o Defense success 95.3%/reject 0% (benign), 44.7%/43.6% (harmful); w/ Defense 94.1%/0% (benign), 9.6%/87.1% (harmful)
Cost analysis: Seed-1.6 achieves 38% safety improvement at $0.19/experience; Claude-4.5-Sonnet multi-turn ASR reduction rises 63%->73% as cost rises $0.16->$1.06

Experience transferability: Claude-4.5-Sonnet-generated experiences give avg 50% (single-turn) / 41% (multi-turn) ASR reduction across executor models; Gemini-3-Flash ASR drops 58% with Claude experiences vs 53% with its own

Repeated-run statistics (temp 0.7, n=3, 100-task subset, Claude-4.5-Sonnet): Single-Turn ASR 43.30%+/-1.25 (temp0: 44.00%), Multi-Turn ASR 88.00%+/-2.16 (temp0: 88.00%), Multi-Turn+ToolShield ASR 16.67%+/-2.62 (temp0: 15.00%)
Bootstrap (1,000 resamples) full-benchmark multi-turn ASR: 72.1%+/-2.3%; by format: Addition 73.2%+/-2.8%, Decomposition 69.4%+/-4.3%

Tool-wise multi-turn degradation and defense effect (Figure 11): Filesystem +21.8%/-22.8%, Playwright +8.2%/-25.5%, Terminal +28.8%/-38.1% (most vulnerable to degradation, most improved by defense), PostgreSQL +13.3%/-37.9%, Notion +14.4%/-26.7%
LLM-judge agreement: 95.15% with rule-based rubrics, 93.53% with human evaluation
74% of tasks that were neither completed nor rejected attributed to technical execution errors rather than safety behavior
baselines_compared: Taxonomy Defense (MAT taxonomy description + seed examples injected into agent context, no self-exploration)
ToolShield w/o simulation (ablation: experiences generated directly from tool documentation, skipping sandboxed execution/summarization/reflection)
w/o Defense (no defense baseline) across all six evaluated models
